student_name: Eliane Portela Nogueira Lima de Brito
hub_subtitle: Business English for a doctor rebuilding her professional voice

lesson.menu_title: Negotiating Like You Mean It — Language for Supplier Meetings
lesson.menu_desc: Modal verbs (could, would, should, might, may) and conditionals for negotiating with American medical-technology suppliers -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 5 -- Negotiating Like You Mean It
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 5 -- Negotiating Like You Mean It

listenings.text: Hi, I am Tom, and I lead sales for a reproductive-technology company here in Texas. Let me tell you how we usually work with a new clinic. First, we send a detailed quote with clear terms and conditions, so there are no surprises later. Our lead time is normally eight to ten weeks, and every device ships with a full warranty and the compliance documents you need for regulatory approval. Now, a quick word of advice for any buyer. Never accept the first number. It is completely normal to ask, what is your best offer, or to say, we would need a faster delivery window. A good vendor will not walk away. We expect a real negotiation. If you ask politely but firmly, you might be surprised how much flexibility there is on price and on the delivery window.

Hi Eliane, this is Karen from the sales team in Austin. Thank you for a great call earlier. I am following up with some good news about your quote. After talking to my manager, we could offer you a better deal. If you confirm the order this month, we would lower the price by ten percent and extend the warranty to seven years. Our lead time stays at eight weeks, and the delivery window is firm. I am also attaching the updated terms and conditions and the compliance documents for regulatory approval. If anything is unclear, you could call me anytime, and we might arrange a short pilot program before the full order. Talk soon, and congratulations on the new clinic.
